methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $25M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $110M - $200M.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Graphyte
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $200M

private.name: Fourth Power
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $150M

private.name: Steady Energy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $110M

data.narrative:
Impulse Labs is a Series A climate and energy company founded by Sam D'Amico that has raised $25M to date. The valuation analysis draws from a comparable universe of 9 private frontier-tech peers in the climate and energy sector, including Graphyte, Fourth Power, and Steady Energy at the same stage, alongside 1 public comparable.

Two triangulation methods were applied. Stage-progression analysis, using total capital raised as a proxy baseline and applying a 2.0x multiplier typical for Series A to subsequent-stage progression, implies a valuation of $50M. Peer-median analysis across three same-sector, same-stage comparables—Graphyte ($200M), Fourth Power ($150M), and Steady Energy ($110M)—yields a median valuation of $150M, with a range of $110M to $200M. The disparity between methods reflects the relative youth of Impulse's funding relative to later-stage peers in the cohort.

The triangulated analysis supports a valuation range of $42M (low) to $172M (high), with a median of $100M. Key uncertainties include the heterogeneity of climate-tech business models at Series A, where capital requirements and time-to-revenue vary significantly, and the relative maturity and progress of peer companies within the $110M–$200M band. Impulse's specific technology readiness, customer traction, and capital efficiency relative to named peers would meaningfully narrow this range.
